entities.name: śabda
entities.cite: Whatever word is uttered does not thereby necessarily partake in the operation

entities.name: sva-rūpa
entities.cite: the same utterance may present its own form or convey another

entities.name: artha
entities.cite: its power to convey something else is not thereby obstructed

entities.name: śakti
entities.cite: The two saktis of 1.55 coexist in every occurrence

entities.name: sva-rūpa-śakti
entities.cite: the same utterance may present its own form or convey another

entities.name: artha-śakti
entities.cite: its power of standing for something beyond itself is not cancelled by its being mentioned

entities.name: vyākaraṇa
entities.cite: not automatically the item to which the taught operation applies

entities.name: paramārtha
entities.cite: its power of standing for something beyond itself

entities.name: vikalpa
entities.cite: which it does in a given sutra is settled by the economy of the system, not by the phonetics

axioms.name: coexist
axioms.cite: The two saktis of 1.55 coexist in every occurrence

axioms.name: aavarana-vimuktah
axioms.cite: its power of standing for something beyond itself is not cancelled by its being mentioned

axioms.name: aavarana-vimuktah
axioms.cite: Use and mention, in modern terms, are not marked on the word's face

axioms.name: dvaya-sakti-sambhava
axioms.cite: the same utterance may present its own form or convey another

axioms.name: na-niyata-karyabhak
axioms.cite: that occurrence is not niyatam karyabhak — not automatically the item to which the taught operation applies

denials.name: exhausts
denials.cite: denying that mention exhausts the word's powers

denials.name: determines-operation
denials.cite: not automatically the item to which the taught operation applies

claims.name: na-niyata-karyabhak
claims.cite: does not thereby necessarily partake in the operation

claims.name: aavarana-vimuktah
claims.cite: its power to convey something else is not thereby obstructed

rejected_readings.label: mention_exhausts_power
rejected_readings.rendering: When a word is mentioned in a rule, its power is exhausted by that mention.
rejected_readings.why: The verse explicitly denies that mention exhausts the word's powers. This reading misses the coexistence doctrine: the word retains both its own-form power and referential power regardless of mention.

rejected_readings.label: utterance_determines_operation
rejected_readings.rendering: A word uttered in a grammatical rule necessarily becomes the direct object of that rule's operation.
rejected_readings.why: Contradicts the core axiom that utterance does not make the word niyatam karyabhak. The verse insists on the independence of the word's powers from its occurrence in a rule.

rejected_readings.label: single_power_doctrine
rejected_readings.rendering: Each word occurrence has a single power determined by its function in the grammatical rule.
rejected_readings.why: Violates the two-powers doctrine central to the verse. The commentary explicitly states the two saktis coexist, and context selects which operates—not that the word has only one power.

notes: The verse embodies the two-powers doctrine (dvaiśakti) central to Bhārtṛhari's theory: every word maintains simultaneous powers to present its own form (sva-rūpa-śakti) and to convey a referent (artha-śakti). The contract captures the hermeneutical corollary: utterance in a rule does not exhaust these powers, nor does it necessarily make the word a direct participant in the operation. Context (vikalpa) determines which power is operative in a given instance.
